text: VO2max declines at approximately 4.0 ml/kg/min per decade in sedentary and active men, and 4.6 ml/kg/min per decade in endurance-trained men, with no statistically significant difference between groups.
population: men across sedentary, active, and endurance-trained populations

text: The relative rate of VO2max decline with age is approximately 6.8-8.7% per decade across training statuses, with no significant differences between sedentary, active, and endurance-trained men.
population: men across sedentary, active, and endurance-trained populations

text: VO2max is strongly inversely correlated with age (r = -0.80 to -0.88) regardless of training status, meaning age-related aerobic decline is unavoidable even with consistent training.
population: men across sedentary, active, and endurance-trained populations

text: Endurance-trained men maintain higher absolute VO2max values than active or sedentary men at any given age, even though the rate of decline is similar.
Maximal heart rate declines with age at a similar rate regardless of training status (r = -0.88 to -0.93 correlation with age).
population: men across sedentary, active, and endurance-trained populations

text: Endurance-trained men show no significant increase in body fat percentage with age, unlike sedentary and active men who gain body fat significantly over time.
Running mileage and speed decline significantly with advancing age in endurance-trained men, which may partly explain the VO2max decline observed in cross-sectional data.
